macro_segments.title: Practices of Avel, Metzora, and Menudeh: Comparison and Distinctions

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Overview of practices for Avel with application to Metzora and Menudeh
Ne'ilat Hasandal (wearing shoes): Avel's prohibition and application to Menudeh and Metzora
Tashmish HaMitah (marital relations): prohibition for Avel and uncertain application to Menudeh and Metzora
Shiluach Korbanos (sending offerings): distinction between Avel, Menudeh, and Metzora
Conceptual framework: societal separation vs. intimate practices; challenge to categorical distinctions

macro_segments.title: Sources and Justifications for Court Powers: Summoning, Contempt, and Ostracization

micro_segments.title: Derivation of court summons procedure from the Korach narrative (משה sending messengers to Datan and Aviram)
Elements of court process: identifying the judge, the litigant, and the defendant in the summons
Multiple summons and time allowances; application to Pharaoh and the concept of shamta (excommunication)
Contempt of court (mispaker): disrespect to court messenger and grounds for excommunication
Escalation framework: shamta, cherem, property seizure (hefker), and corporal punishment
Derivation of cherem and property seizure from Ezra narrative; rationale for progression of remedies
Court authority for corporal punishment and execution (Hardafah): emergency powers and scope

macro_segments.title: Timeline and Process of Niduy: Warning, Imposition, Escalation, and Release Mechanisms

micro_segments.title: Niduy imposition timing: immediate for contempt vs. Monday-Thursday-Monday warning for civil matters
Escalation sequence: thirty days niduy, second period, then cherem after sixty days
Case study: the butcher and Rabbi Tuvi bar Masna; whether niduy can be shortened upon compliance
Debate over early release from niduy: same court requirement vs. ability of another court to release

macro_segments.title: Reciprocal Obligations and Hierarchy in Niduy: Personal vs. Communal Authority

micro_segments.title: Niduy to Rav vs. talmid: hierarchy of recognition and applicability across communities
Rabban Gamliel's dictum: personal niduy tied to individual; death does not automatically release
Three inferences from Rabban Gamliel: personal niduy status, individual release authority, three-person requirement
Personal niduy (l'chavodo) vs. institutional niduy: different release requirements and scope
Amoraim dispute: Amemar permits different court to release; Rav Ashi challenges using Rabban Gamliel
Resolution: death does not release; another three-person court must convene for release authority
